utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, data-driven reports that satisfy all six evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills is marginally more effective for the following reasons: (1) It explicitly invokes the skill's formal plateau threshold (<0.5 kg / 2-week criterion) as a named command (/fitness:weightloss-plateau-check), making the clinical standard explicit rather than implicit. (2) The daily pass/fail analysis (Days ≥500 kcal Deficit column showing 5/7 for weeks 1-6 and 0/7 for weeks 7-8) is more granular and directly satisfies J4. (3) The skill-structured output (using /fitness:weightloss-bmr, /nutrition:weightloss-track namespaces) demonstrates methodological rigor. That said, the wo_skills agent independently performed all the same computations using Python scripting and produced a similarly complete, numerically grounded report. The quality difference is modest — wo_skills is surprisingly strong, scoring 1 on all items — but wi_skills edges it out on formal criterion application and structured deficit pass/fail analysis. Conclusion: wi_skills is slightly more effective.

utility_details.judge_error: Neither wi_skills nor wo_skills used the Katch-McArdle formula with lean body mass to recalculate BMR; both used Mifflin-St Jeor, yielding ~1227 kcal/day instead of the expected ~1330 kcal/day. As a consequence, neither derived the correct maintenance TDEE of ~1829 kcal/day or the 1750-1900 kcal/day maintenance band. Additionally, neither document explicitly verified safety floors in the final output file. The wi_skills run created an unexpected file in /app/data/health-logs/2026-05/2026-05-04.json as a side effect of the /nutrition:weightloss-track command.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is modestly more effective than wo_skills, scoring 4/7 versus wo_skills scoring 3/7. The wi_skills run invoked the skill's /fitness and /nutrition command namespaces (weightloss-setup, weightloss-bmr, weightloss-report, weightloss-plateau-check), produced richer body-composition framing, and correctly referenced the abdominal-risk threshold (J5 pass). Both runs correctly confirmed goal achievement (J1) and quantified a 60 kg early-warning trigger (J6), and both quantified the deficit-to-maintenance calorie step-up (J7). However, both failed on J2 (Katch-McArdle BMR not used), J3 (correct TDEE band not derived), and J4 (safety floor not explicitly verified in output). The key differentiator is J5: wi_skills mentioned the abdominal-risk threshold while wo_skills omitted it entirely. Overall, neither run fully met the domain-specific standard set by the expected results, but wi_skills demonstrated more structured, skill-driven analysis.
